commentBody: Easy breezy Friday!  Must have been on the creator’s wavelength as I finished in 1/3 my average Friday time!
Thank God It's Tuesday.....

Absolutely charming themeless that is to me a perfect example of why Wednesday should be a swing day, sometimes with a simple theme, sometimes with a less than tricky trick, and sometimes with a gentle themeless.
Thanks for the fun, Rena.
Way too easy for a Friday. Not a PB but close . . .

I probably won't be the first to say that locks are also the wishbone-shaped things on the edges of boats that you can stick your oars in. I probably won't be the first to say that I got a Friday personal best, either. The trivia just might've been on my wavelength thought; the only clue that seemed notably too easy to me was [Dorm buddy]. An enjoyable puzzle, at any rate!

The NYT Crossword drops at 7:30 AM for me (8:30 AM when Daylight Savings Time either began or ended, I don't understand it at all)  and I like to first read the puzzle introduction in the Gameplay Column, then begin solving and revisit if I'm stuck at a tricky clue.

I remember reading Mr Shortz' introduction to Rena's Sunday debut in January which talked about her originally hearing Mr Shortz speak at an event and sending him her first crossword submission at age 9.

Today's introduction, like that one, is very heartening. I'm rooting for Rena!

"I might get a rear-mounted device on my car."
"Hey, no spoilers!"

("But my racer's the torque of the town!")
Well that’s one of the easiest Fridays in a while, but I’m not complaining. It was super fun!
pretty!

Straight from the hip Out West:

Armadillos are some tough critters. They can run fast, scramble and make a mess outta your back yard.

If it were not for the spray, I'd rather run into a skunk that creep up on an armadillo. They'll hiss and scream sooner than run, and who wants to see them jump three feet in the air on a dark night!

And don't get me started about them running around like little armored trucks under my back deck! I thank them for helping themselves to all the grubs and bugs they care to snuffle, but they can vamoose from my veg patch!

PS - Happy Weekend y'all.
Fridays should offer resistance.  This offered very little.  Agree with Barry, good puzzle for a Wednesday.

Add me to the personal best Friday time list! Delightful, fun puzzle, the longer spanners clicked with very few crossings which made the rest fall into place nicely. 

Thanks, Rena!
The metal brackets that hold oars in place in a row boat are called, “oarlocks.”

While everyone notes their fast solving times (sometimes with implied disapproval of the "too easy" Friday crossword), I'm going to mention my streak: 

1461, which I believe is exactly four years. (365 x 4 + one Leap Day).

And like many commenters, I solved it well below my average Friday time, but I enjoyed it a lot.  Nice set of entries!
I had GAMS for baguettes, not knowing about the gems.  It makes a kind of sense when you think about it, and are as old as I am.  They are long and slender after all.
In continuation of my comment from yesterday, I spent New Year's in South Africa this year. A very popular dish there is BOERewors, meaning farmer's sausage. Wouldn't have gotten that clue otherwise

This was a kind of frustrating puzzle.  A few clues I really appreciated like THE MUNCHIES, SPOILER ALERTS, CIA ASSET,  ARMADILLOS, etc.

But also a LOT of obscure trivia type of answers and proper nouns.  Stuff like OLIN, MASA, AEROBE, BOER.  Words that if you don't have specific knowledge they aren't guessable or even figureoutable letter sequences.  It made top left especially tough cause I originally had IN ROAD instead of ON A LOG for the bump and COMEDY instead of ROMCOM for Crazy Rich Asians.  So though LOCK AND LOAD and OBES were more reasonable clues they were tough to spot.

I'd call this puzzle equal parts fun and frustrating.

Nice puzzle but not for a Friday. I’m sure I’m like many who look forward to Fridays and Saturdays, when I can settle in, enjoy the challenge and take it slow. I look forward to, first, that moment after the first pass through when I’m staring at empty squares and think I have no chance of solving the puzzle and, then, that moment when things start to click. This puzzle provided neither of those moments, as nice as it is. I don’t want my Friday solve to be over in 15 minutes.

A few of my co-workers tease me about my failure to get cultural references. I'm happy to acknowledge this weakness l, and also to say it was no obstacle to solving this puzzle. In fact, as my time was almost half my Friday average, you could say it was made for someone like me!
Pumping my arms with excitement! Sixteen minutes faster than my average Friday. I’m a genius. Don’t take that away from me people. Big smile over here.
What are they going to do for the Easy Mode Friday puzzle😭

I liked this puzzle. Short and sweet. Thought I might be looking for answers with multiple double letters when COLLEGE ROOMMATE and CIA ASSET popped up, but I guess there was no real theme. I also applaud ERADICATE for "completely destroy" instead of the all-too-common (and incorrect) "decimate". But I've made peace with that redefinition. Sigh.
1A has nothing to do with canal locks. The C-shaped swiveling fixtures on the gunwales of a rowboat, which hold the oars in position, are called "oar locks".

What a lovely puzzle! It had me feeling like I was in the flow, in the zone, and in zen, from the moment I got a toehold. Lovely as it was, I must agree with @Barry Ancona that it would have been better situated as a Wednesday, if only Wednesday would become a "swing" day, as he calls it. Whether themeless or gimmicky, easier puzzles would fit nicely in an all-encompassing Wednesday. That said I just loved the misdirects in this puzzle and the way they showcase Ms. Cohen's talent for construction and cluing. [Plant overseas, perhaps] for CIA AGENTS was brilliant, but in my heart of hearts I really wanted the answer to be Robert... Here he is, with Alison Krauss, in a phase of his that has nothing to do with Led Zep, but is just as great: https://youtu.be/L3Xi5gvZ7Kk?si=w9KADpf_NDHkOTRQ Thank you Ms. Cohen! It was a great solving ride indeed!
Greetings from BENIS, DN!
Very chewy Friday puzzle.  One where nothing is hitting at the beginning but slow, incremental progress eventually led to a satisfying success.  Perfect level for a Friday IMO.

I don’t know if the mob will say this was easy or hard, but it was such a delight. I’ll read commentary later, but the entries were so refreshing and fun (to me). Coupled with really fun, thoughtful cluing. 

THE MUNCHIES?   PUNSTERS?  SPOILER ALERT?  COLD TAKES?  LOCK AND LOAD?
this is was inspired. 

I feel fortunate to have been given this puzzle. 
Thank you Rena. This was an OPUS.

Rena Cohen's crosswords go down so smoothly to me, without awkward crossword-ese. I like to see real phrases that people actually use, like THEMUNCHIES and LOCKANDLOAD. (Though ever since I watched the Inside No. 9 episode about cryptic crosswords, I can't see NINA without wanting to look for a nina.)

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
A Weintraubian Wednesday.
I don't care how fresh and breezy it is; Fridays shouldn't take me 11 minutes.

I basically had to guess my way out of several sections of the puzzle, and yet I finished it in Wednesday time. Weird. Apparently, paradoxically, the grid was bith too easy and too hard for me.

I have never vibed with Rena Cohen, and today was no different. She seems to have a penchant for relying on idioms, words and meanings of words I don't know. The "not knowing" part is on me, of course, but the fact remains it makes for personally unpleasant solves.

The column should really have specified that baguettes are gem *cuts* rather than gems, as such. I refused to believe there was a gemstone I've never heard of, and sure enough, a quick online search confirmed it's the name of a gem *cut*. "Gem" is not a synonym of a "gem cut", is it? Even if "gems" may denote cut gemstones, specifically.

One thing I still don't get: how are MISSES a section of a dress shop?
Let’s just say that I was on the same wavelength, and leave it at that.

I enjoyed SPOILERALERT and THEMUNCHIES and pretty underwhelmed by everything else. Cute grid, I suppose, but filled in almost as fast as I could type. Lena OLIN and Elizabeth OLSEN are like OREOS these days, it seems. Oh well tomorrow is another day

If I remember correctly, I encountered the word "baguette" back in my teens when reading "The Three Musketeers", and yes, it was describing the cut of the gems rather than the gems. With a three-letter answer, GEM was the only thing that fit.

This was a very easy puzzle for a Friday, but I still might have had to look up a few answers (actors) but for some helpful crosses. Having been in warehouse stores when knife companies were doing demos and giveaways, I wanted ShOppER ALERTS, but the SPOILER version was better. Having helped my wife put math textbooks into braille, THETA was readily available instead of being a lost memory. The source of the ATARI name was also a remembered fact.

As a first themeless effort, I'm not going to be critical about the lack of difficulty, The puzzle flowed nicely, had some interesting trivia and some answers where I had to depend on crosses, and was a pleasant solve. Thanks, Rena!
Definitely felt like a themeless Wednesday. But it was just under my Friday best, so it made me feel smarter than I actually am, so thanks I guess

A very easy Friday. My Wednesdays sometimes last longer 😂

The fill was fun. The only part I disliked was the cross between LOCKANDLOAD/MASA/DOOLEY. I didn't exactly know either of these and because two letters cross it wasn't even guessable. I had to do a reveal at the end. Also because I was not sure ONALOG is correct or not, could have well been ONADOG or ONACOG as I was unaware of the idiom.

I spent the afternoon getting my flower beds in shape from a pretty harsh winter (no planting yet - we have a frost alert tonight) so I spent a long time on "plant overseas". It wasn't until it was completely filled in from crosses I realized I was so misdirected. From the comments it seems like it was an easy clue for everyone else.

Hi! Regular reader, new commentator. 

This puzzle was breezy. So fun! Turtle rabbit at 17 across and the 32 across clues made my week. Thanks for a great puzzle! And thanks to all you regulars for helping me with my last sticky entries! I strive to be like you!

Has anyone already pointed out the error in the column concerning oars and locks? In this case, “locks” does not refer to chambers of a canal, but to oarlocks, the Y-shaped pieces of iron alloy that connect the oars to the gunwales.

I just did Thursday and Friday back to back, in under 40 minutes. That’s some big wheelhouse I’m building, or these were pretty easy. Not complaining, though. I enjoyed both. (Although Thursday’s flew by so fast I didn’t even notice the hooks until I read the comments. That shouldn’t happen, right?)
I just realized that PUNSTER rhymes with Munster, Mike.
Easy peasy…. The Wales clue was straightforward as I’m doing the puzzle in a cafe in Cardiff!

I like to think I can obtain a meta-level while working on a puzzle.

That is, I'm analyzing how I'm going about doing the puzzle.  And I saw something tonight which seems to be happening a lot more.

I looked at [Home of Swansea and Wrexham] and immediately thought WALES.  But then somehow I dismissed it and put in essEx.  Then a cross eliminated that, but I never went back and really re-evaluated.  I finally had _ALES and it *still* took me a several seconds to come up *with my original guess*.

It's a good thing my brain and I go way back together.  I can't stay mad.

Crazy crucivibing, Batman!

Rena- you sent this out of the park.

It made me feel giddy, challenged,
and even a wee bit clever!

And an ACPT puzz to boot.

How’s *that* for a start to my day?

THE MUNCHIES crossing MAI TAI?
LOCK AND LOAD?

COUPLES ONLY,
PUNSTERS…

And the (almost) non-existent crosswordese
clued beautifully [ Some winds ]?

GEM after gem.
Swooning.

CC is swooning.

Pardon me while I swoon away…

SPOILERALERT: I thought this was a fine puzzle, although not quite the chewy Friday I long for.  

DOOLEY was a flat out gimme for me.  My father, despite not having an ounce of musical ability himself, loved music, primarily classical, and especially opera.  But he also had a weakness for the folk music of the fifties and early sixties.  Thanks to him, I was weaned on music from The Kingston Trio, The Weavers, Pete Seeger, Joan Baez, Odetta, The Clancy Brothers, and Judy Collins.  As I got older I developed a taste for many other kinds of music that he had no interest in, but to this day, I never lost my love of those early  influences and I’m most apt to listen to folky, singer/songwritery types.
What is the significance of accepting a score of zero at a crossword tournament (in that it caused a burst of applause)?

The LOG saying is funny, although not heard of it. I was thinking of pregnancy ...
Out of curiosity, again. Do US students still share rooms? Even when I was a student (1971!) it was rare, and only chosen because it was cheaper. Unknown now. 
I remember the Tom Dooley song, it gave me the shivers when I was young.

Random thoughts:
•	PUNSTERS crossing ART. There are puns that make you roll your eyes and want to run away, and there are others that make you shake your head at how good they are.  To produce the latter is indeed an art.  (Hi, @Mike!)
•	Amazing that THE MUNCHIES has never appeared before not only in the Times puzzle, but in all the major outlets.  
•	Impressive how junk-scarce this low 68-word, low 30 black square puzzle is.  CAPABLE indeed.  Truly – try making one of these sometimes.
•	SONOROUS is gorgeous and ARMADILLO is sonorous.
•	Don’t ask me why, but MILLET made Edna St. Vincent Millay pop into my head, prompted me to look her up, resulting in a terrific TIL, that she was the first woman to win a Pulitzer in poetry.
•	ON A LOG was a lovely reminder of Monday’s ANTS ON A LOG.
•	LOOFA crossing a backward TOOFA! 

Your puzzle, Rena, was a box full of lovely.  Thank you for making it!
Loved the puzzle, Rachel. It was fun to solve with some sparkling clues. Really enjoyed it!
Nice one.  The little grey cells thank you.
Breezed through it in 6:45

A bump on a log isn’t very antsy, so no peanut butter or Cheezwiz needed.

A “storybook” Friday? I can hardly make myself believe this is where we have come to.

Years ago, when I was still into my guitar-playing and Latin America folk and altiplano music, my then mother-in-law was about to embark on a trip to South America. I begged her to bring be back a charango, a typical Andean instrument made from the back of an armadillo. And she did. I was able to find an instruction book called Método de Charango, and tried to teach myself to play it. I didn't put a lot of effort into it, and life got in the way. 

I still have the charango, sitting on my coffee table. But I just learned from a post made hours ago here that armadillos can transmit leprosy. Yikes! These days, charangos are made solely from wood, maybe for that reason. On my own trip to Peru and Bolivia about 10 years ago, I never came across one made from an armadillo.

A fine Friday puzzle, IMHO.  Perhaps a bit too breezy, but the fill was so fresh that I find it hard to complain.
LOCK AND LOAD, THE MUNCHIES, SONOROUS, PUNSTERS, FREE PR.  All welcome inclusions.
And some lovely cluing: "Plant overseas", "Announcements before giveaways?", and "Aztec name that translates to 'turtle-rabbit'" to name a few.

My one nit:  "Dorm buddy" is way to easy a clue for a Friday spanner.  I slapped in COLLEGE ROOMMATE with just one or two crossings.

Thanks for a nice Friday, Rena Cohen!

Statement. I didn't do very well on this puzzle. That's an understatement. Anyway - puzzle find today - a Sunday from October 30, 2011 by Andrea Carla Michaels and Patrick Blindauer with the title: "Hollywood from left to right." Will just list a few theme answers: ANGELMANAGEMENT THELIGHTSTUFF PILATESOFTHECARIBBEAN FANTASTICFOUL TAXIDRIVEL WEDDINGCLASHERS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/30/2011&g=23&d=A See you tomorrow. ....

Far, far too easy for a Friday.

This is getting seriously annoying. It's like I signed up for a marathon, but when I got there they only made me run a block. There is zero sense of accomplishment.

Look, the clue about ARMADILLOS was fun and all, but this was not a Friday-level puzzle. The occasional fun or tricky clue does not make up for a whole puzzle full of gimmes.

No offense is meant to Rena Cohen. There was a lot to like about this puzzle. But the editors are obviously making a conscious choice to dumb down the crossword, and I do not appreciate it. We have the Easy Mode clues for Fridays, isn't that enough? Where are the puzzles for people who want a challenge?

Took me a bit to find my way into this but once I did it went fairly smoothly and I finished in around a third of my average time. Enjoyed the clueing for 12 A (“Announcements before giveaways,” for SPOILER ALERTS), 38A (“Satellite alternative,” for AMFM), and I also liked learning that the Mayan name for ARMADILLOS translates to “turtle-rabbit”  and that ATARI came from a term from Go.

A personal story about 21D (Tom DOOLEY): As a young kid in the ‘60s, before I ever really listened to the lyrics of the song, I thought it was about the (since disgraced) medical missionary Thomas Dooley, who’d gone to med school with my father at St. Louis University, and about whom my father often told funny stories, usually having to do with his apparent indifference to the rules, which meant he didn’t graduate with my father and the rest of the class of ‘52, but took another year. Back then, before the information came out about his collaborating with the CIA, he was a hero, in the country at large and most certainly in our household. I used to think, “Wow! They even wrote a song about him.”

What a beautiful grid!  Lots of long answers -- and all of them are chewy and colorful and unusual.  From COLD TAKES to SPOILER ALERTS to COUPLES ONLY to MAKE BELIEVE to LOCK AND LOAD to CIA ASSET to THE MUNCHIES -- why you could practically write a novel based on these phrases and it would be a really, really, really interesting novel.  You wouldn't even need to take a fiction-writing class; these phrases practically write the novel for you.

The wonderful thing about SONOROUS is that it's such a sonorous word.  It's my second favorite word after SERENDIPITY -- also a sonorous word.  For me to truly love a word, it has to have a lovely sound as well as a lovely meaning.

To those constructors who LOCK AND LOAD their grids with pop names and other crosswordese, study what Rena has done with hers.  It's a beauty!

We asked for an uptick in difficulty, and you delivered!  
Once I gave up on the CIA *AGENT* things were simpler...  Some very nice vocabulary in this puzzle.
I had a hard time with the actors, device, sports items (as usual) and COLD FACTS slowed me down.  It has been so very long since I watched Project Runway that NINA Garcia was almost declared MiA....
My fave clue was for 17A...having seen some ARMADILLOS up close and personal, I  agree with the Aztecs.  (These mammals are really quite cute.)
So we think Mike from Munster recognized himself at 33D?

This came out in a gush for me. I was thinking "keys" for 1A, but then I looked at 1D and I was off. 
Had goodPR before FREEPR.
Wondered, as I always do, whether it was OLSoN or OLSEN. Someday that will stick.
Even though I know nothing about proteins, I appreciated the clue for 53A, because it was fresh. 
With all the shake-ups in college conferences, I did wonder briefly if UNC was still in the ACC.
Happy Friday, everyone!
For 'sticks in locks' I had DAMS crossing with MOMCOM. Both of which I felt made sense! The wordplay column gave me that one "key" that I needed to "unlock" the win!

33 minutes faster than average for the Gold Star, so not complaining.  Had Cilantro for overseas plant for a bit.  

Funny.
Fun puzzle! Although I was sad to learn that my (punny?) guess of PUNSTARS was incorrect.
Too easy or not, this makes my first-ever six day streak. If I can solve tomorrow's puzzle I can die a happy man.
re: sticks in locks - The things that keep oars in place for rowing are referred to as "oar locks".

I usually find the Bonus Puzzle themes a bit twee, but today;s is a banger. Maybe not so much for fans of the Kingston Trio.

I had their "Best of" album as a kid, but somehow never wondered why Tom DOOLEY killed that girl...until today. Sad but true story. No spoilers.

I love this puzzle.  Thanks to Rena Cohen for a satisfying solve. It was pleasing in all areas-composition, cluing, delightful misdirection, and multiple layers. She's now on my favorite constructors list!

Lovely candidate (as clued) for a themeless Tuesday puzzle. Quite the letdown to be done in just 12 minutes---my fastest ever Friday solve, and I'm not a fast solver.

Editorial mistake today? The new normal, to increase revenue? Whatever, the effect is the same. 

Puzzle itself was fine.
A pretty quick solve to a dead end, as I thought that “gams” was a clever answer to “baguettes”, which is idiomatic in French for legs.

Well, that was fun! I don’t remember the last time I solved a Friday without lookups, but today I scored my best Friday ever at 23:57, a full 19:13 better than my average! Got really stuck in the SW corner, but eventually remembered SONOROUS (32D) and the rest finally came together! Whoo-hoo! 🎉
I wasn’t sure about the dutch farmer, then I remembered the german “bauer” from my euchre playing days - just had to shorten it down to 4 letters!
Well, that was easy! Half my average time. Somehow, it all just clicked! Thanks for a fun puzzle.

Especially sparkling puzzle AND  column 😊

I thought I'd need to look up something to get the southeast corner unstuck but in the end, the crosses solved it. Think I'll  go look up something anyhow so I can learn some new back story about the clues I didn't know - one of the side joys of puzzling!

Happy Friday, everyone!
For everyone complaining about the difficulty level of the puzzles, keep in mind that they can only use the puzzles they receive.  If you want a more difficult puzzle, construct one and submit it.

Got bogged down for a while in various cardinal directions, but I've been working on reading different clues at the same time with each eye so I was able to bring it in under my 142.34 second average. Hopefully tomorrow will be more of a challenge.
A "heat" is not really part of a race.  It's a race in itself.

Late puzzle find. One of the most amazing I've ever seen. A Sunday from February 29(!), 2004 by Patrick Merrell with the title: LEAPDAY There was a solid black bar across the middle of the puzzle, and four down theme answers that jumped over that bar. Those answers: HOPSACROSS_THEBORDER JUMPSFROM_SIDETOSIDE SKIPDOWNTO_THEBOTTOM ATTEMPTTO_PASSTHEBAR Thought that was just amazing. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=2/29/2004 ...

Very quick. 

I think the clue for 1A was my favorite, because I could think of keys and oars--but also pins in a tumbler lock. And "stick" could refer to the shape or to what you do with it, although you would not normally refer to a key as something that "sticks in locks." 

Also was misdirected for a little bit by the answer for "A craving for snacks" starting off with THEM___.

Would love to know what Rena's original clues were.
I loved this puzzle. Enough misdirection along with clever clues that make your brain think differently. Only 35 seconds over average. Got the MIDI in 1:41, so a decent puzzle day all around.

COLLEGEROOMMATE was not the gimme for me as it was for some of you... my first roommate hated me for no reason I have ever been able to fathom. It was from the get-go, so I don't know what the problem was... it's always bothered me a bit.  

Literally, the only time she ever spoke to me was to accuse me of something I didn't do. Alas, my high hopes for a fun roomie situation were quickly dashed. Fear not, I made a lot of other buddies, and remain close to some of them to this day. 

As for the puzzle, fun, fast, and fresh!

This one stands out for its delightfully high proportion of clues that seem to mean one thing but really mean something else.  And I am here for each and every one of them!

I had trouble with the upper left section until I thought more about BAGUETTES and remembered that also refers to jewelry, and so I slid GEMS in there and that was enough to give me traction to finish the whole puzzle.

Really delightful, thank you!

Not to pile on, but I will add my voice to the chorus saying it was a pretty swell puzzle but too easy for a Friday. This is especially frustrating since Friday is the day the NYT publishes an easy version of the clues for those who are still learning and/or want less of a challenge. Please give us something to sink our teeth into at the end of the week.
Another fun Midi that you need to have Show Overlays on to fully appreciate.

I did enjoy this puzzle and I felt like the name seemed familiar! So Rena Cohen had a puzzle in the ACPT. Good notes too by the way, Sean!
#GoodTimes

This was a satisfying fill. Challenging for me for quite a while as I just was not on the same wavelength as the constructor. 

Rating:
8/10

Just a question: I finished Wednesday just this morning after Thursday and Friday had come out and I only got the Blue Star even though I didn't go for any help. I think I've read before of this happening. Is this something new?
To my mind, a HEAT is a RACE. just not the final race. If it's a part of anything, it's a part of a MEET.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzz

a/ who comes say i
in storybook slang
to scruff and skulk the shadows
with sticks and locks
and go before the winds…
thou rabbit philosopher
thou bitter craving
for animal’s talks
d/ of lovers, a king, and the trillion opinions-
the babbler’s song
hit-hit the body
kong…kong…
and a/ make the make-believe

About 24 years ago, I defended my PhD dissertation related to myosin and ACTIN. What a surprise to see this in a NYT puzzle. A nice flashback to a different era. And even further back in time: how about THEMUNCHIES while playing ATARI!

WHALES is spelled with an H.

😉
Very,  very cool Midi today 🌷

This was fun and I found it just a bit more accessible than yesterday's. Or at least I needed more help with yesterday's. I often need to look at the column and some of the Tricky Clues on late week puzzles. I don't consider it cheating under my rules for myself. I'm certainly pretty hopeless with math and sports for sure. The one that gave me a good chuckle was "Bump's place"
Thanks for the fun and the challenge Rena Cohen.

A personal best at 13:43. Love the long crosses - somehow they jump out at me with only a letter or two. Today was day 362 of my streak. I have promised myself that I will quit at 365, except to do Thursday through an occasional Sunday, and with no pressure to complete the on release day. Happy for a personal best on the last Friday of what will be my longest streak ever.
Took me a while to figure out baguettes weren't, like GaMS, a slang term for legs.

I can’t believe Leon Botstein has to retire(as well as that fundraiser for the Whitney having to be apologetic in the NYT),  while a certain person we know who was deemed too impatient to be allowed in the situation room during a recent delicate military extraction, does not. These two names and many others were leaked on purpose to create a distraction while you know who was blacked out 1,000,000 times!
Pretty easy for a Friday but mostly enjoyable. More science trivia than usual which actually helped me

Just did this Monday’s New Yorker puzzle from Will Nediger, and I found it unusually tough (in terms of recent New Yorkers) and very much enjoyed solving it.

I don’t usually pay too much attention to grids…but Nediger’s is a really cool one! Beyond just looking nice, I found that it broke the solve up in an interesting way.

The puzzle also has a couple borderline troll-y entries (for example, I instantly knew what 35A was going for, but laughed out loud at being asked to provide a definitive spelling).

Recommended!

Fridays have become my favorite puzzle.  I'm enjoying these themeless puzzles more and more as the themed revelers have gotten cluttered of late.

I enjoyed this one.  It wasn't overly difficult.  I just took some patience to solve.
Re 1A : Locks are also bodies of water where one might find oars. 😊
Have heard plenty of hot takes, never heard of COLD TAKES. But only got stuck cause I misspelled AEROBE.
I really thought it was PUN STARS and im disappointed that it’s not

Wonderful puzzle, Rena. 

Wrong day of the week, editors.
@John Just under my average Tuesday time….

@jam
Don't do that. Life is confusing enough.
@jam Delaware represent!

Jake,
I don't time my solves, and I don't care about anyone else's time. Whatever pace is right for you is right for you. Median solve time for a bunch of solvers to do one Friday compared to other Fridays does offer some objective measure of the relative ease or difficulty of a puzzle. Beyond that, I could care less.
@Barry Ancona https://youtu.be/FpVpIaC6QrM?si=ECRFU8pg_J3sD58D
I agree,  It was personal best for me.
Agree.  Tuesday-easy.  Did this one get published with the "Easy Mode" clues, or what?  Lovely puzzle, but not Friday difficulty.
Kimble, Actually, you were the first to "offer an alternative" to our columnist's suggestion that [locks] for OARS are part of a canal. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/oarlock

@Kimble

And I think that's what the constructor was actually referring to.  You need oarlocks on a rowboat, but you don't necessarily need a canal.

Steve,
Actually, you don't need oarlocks, but that's a thole other story.

@Aradhana

For Daylight Savings Time, I always have to revisit the refrain "Spring forward, Fall back". It's a very helpful memory device, even though I've been turning clocks forward and back my whole life!

@G

It's not Daylight Savings Time, it's Daylight Saving Time.  No s on the end of Savings.
@Doug - And the Book of Revelation doesn't have an "s" but most of us call it Revelations.    And the word "anyway" is often said "anyways".   In real life  "s" happens quite often.

@  Slowly, individual Canadian provinces are opting out of changing the clocks twice a year.  Saskatchewan was first to adopt year-round standard time 60 years ago.  More recently, British Columbia, Alberta, the Northwest Territories and Yukon Territories are opting for year-round daylight savings time.

@Mike
When you get up to speed, you've got to stay grounded, but at a good price—find the wind in your sales.

@Mike

You were the first person I thought of when I solved PUNSTERS

Death, taxes, and Mike - who shows up everyday to make us laugh and forget about the first two. 

Thanks, buddy!
@Mike Even at the best of times we can’t keep up with your turbo-charged pun machine!

@Mike
I hope you can a Ford it.  It will be a Kia asset for your driving.

@Whoa Nellie

PSS - Great puzzle! Thanks, Rena Cohen.

@Whoa Nellie

PSSS - Loving Sean McGowan Wordplay column. Very fresh.

@Al  in Pittsburgh

Not the place to express opinion on animal research, so will just paste The Google bit:

"Armadillos are currently the primary model for studying how leprosy causes nerve damage, helping researchers pilot new neuroprotective therapies"

The varmints carry leprosy bacteria similar to humans, and yes, unfortunately, they can transmit leprosy to humans.

Being (mostly) vegetarian, the loss of armadillo stew or "turtle rabbit" friccassee does not leave a rut in my gut.

@Whoa Nellie
And Google's source is?

@SBK in TO

A quick Google search will find you many sources ranging from scholarly papers to the CDC. They are a natural reservoir for leprosy.
@Whoa Nellie One of my favorite random factoids is that armadillos almost always give birth to identical quadruplets!

@Wyra
Thanks for checking. I am uncomfortable accepting "Google says" as a meaningful statement any more, as the AI slop oozes over everything.

@Al in Pittsburgh armadillos got it from humans and are now a source of transmission back to humans (but very rarely). See https://source.colostate.edu/humans-gave-leprosy-to-armadillos-now-they-are-giving-it-back-to-us/ for one explanation
@Nick close to a PB while not particularly focused (simultaneously eating my dinner leisurely).

@Nancy Niemczyk

Not to be confused with warlocks.

@Nancy Niemczyk Funny, in British English they’re called “rowlocks”, which very usefully rhymes with “bollocks”. The latter is quite rude in English (referring to a pendulous pairing in the male anatomy) but is not part of the American lexicon (AFAIK) - hence i dare to write it here.

@Petrol

In the US, we have altered the spelling a bit to use the word in just one set expression: to "bollix" up something is to make a total mess of it.
@Nancy Niemczyk Or 'rollocks' (rowlocks), not to be confused with boll**ks!

@SBK in TO

“Were there grids that almost pushed you into a lookup?”

Of course I don’t know anything about @DYT’s solving practices, but I’ve gotten the impression that most people who discuss their streaks in this forum allow themselves the occasional “lookup” — but using google rather than the app’s cheating functionality.

That said, if DYT has a genuine 1461 day streak going (i.e. no external assistance of any kind), I’m not sure why they’d put “too easy” in quotes for today’s puzzle…
@Infidel - I was also stuck on GAMS for the same reason until I saw your comment

@Infidel

I tried gams first, too. It was my last square.
I remembered learning about the Boer War, but did not know the 'farmer' meaning.
@Chris I think that's why I loved it so much!

@Chris I guessed BOER. I figured there must be a reason we could be expected to get it, so what "farmer" might a Dutch person be that would possibly be known internationally...?
I'd never remotely heard of BUMP ON A LOG either, which did make the NW corner tricky. Looking it up it's quite a nice idiom though - someone who's quiet & unengaged, just sitting there like a bump on a log... I might try and remember to use it!

@Alex

As a child, often when I would complain about being bored, one or the other of my parents would tell me to stop sitting  around like a bump on a log, and do something.  This was often followed by, if you can't find anything to do, I can find something for you.  That always got this bump off the log.

@Chris

At least, this "ON A LOG" didn't include ants.
@Alex Out of curiosity, how did you come to BOER? There was no way, in my mind, to know that unless you just *know* it.
@D - The Dutch colonizers of South Africa were largely farmers.  So the Dutch colonizers were known as BOERs.  It's more world history knowledge than Dutch language knowledge for many of us.

@Chris - I'm surprised that neither you nor the responders are familiar with South African history and the major role of the Boers and the Boer War (although surely Steve L. is, but didn't mention it.)

@Caroline Kearney

Thanks for your confidence in me. And you are indeed correct.

@Dave Rosenbaum
That's exactly the way a Friday should be, one that ultimately provides a sense of accomplishment. For the first three-quarters of this one, it felt like a Monday. The SE stuck me for about half a minute, but then it didn't. Filling in the blanks like an automaton doesn't do it, not for a Friday. It would have been a delight early in the week.

@FWIW

Yipee! Feels great, innit?

I just replied to another post about how I don't like people who feel a need to inform me how long it took to solve the puzzle, and I noted that you asked that no one rain on your parade, as they say.

Your uncurbed enthusiasm is most welcome! What I hate is a statistic.

Wait a minute while I ERADICATE the thick crust of sarcasm which usually accompanies my use of the word I want to say.
Ok, done.

Congratulations!
@VN The clue did not say “canal locks”.  It said “sticks in locks.”

@Nick

VN’s comment is in response to text from the column, which said the answer referred to the oars of a rowboat in the locks of a canal.  

I came here to post the same as what VN has said so I’ll leave it at that 🤣

Mark
@VN. Here they are row locks, but still the same comment
@Nick the column explained it as “canal locks”.
@VN  The Times has noted your comment and made a correction.

@Bay Area Native
Thank you. I've been checking the "from NYT" comments for a face palm, but thanks to you I know the correction is in the actual wordplay column at the end.

@sotto voce I never usually click on links shared in the comments, but when you mentioned Plant & Krauss I hoped it would be that song, so I clicked and it was! Beautiful one, haven't listened to it in years - thanks!
@sotto voce Thank you for the treat!

@sotto voce
I have this album, it's wonderful.

@sotto voce Love the Plant/Krauss collaborations. Though I must admit I find watching videos of live performances more interesting. Can't believe the first album came out in 2007. Seems like yesterday. This post reminded that the Times Magazine did a survey of musicians and others to come up with a list of what they are calling "the 30 greatest living American songwriters," which you and others might find interesting. Fun, interesting, some outrage. Anyway here's a free link. https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2026/magazine/greatest-american-songwriters-alive.html?unlocked_article_code=1.fFA.C2Cq.y8jNNmSTsol9&smid=url-share There are also interviews with some of the people on the list. If you do a search for songwriter in the websites index, they should come up. I'm still working on digesting it all.

@sotto voce

Fantastic song.

You gave my 
already-lovely morning
a soundtrack!

I appreciate you!

@sotto voce
ASSETS

@tricia

Yes! Oops...thanks for the correction!

@Alex You're so welcome! @MB My pleasure! @Jane W I have the album, too, and I agree it's wonderful. As Robert Plant himself tells it, Alison Krauss had to teach him how to control his voice and sing, haha. @Vaer Yes, I agree that live performance videos are better, and I apologize that I only searched for one just now. Here it is: https://youtu.be/Fr4v-bq87Yo?si=mVk0-VY0VdUmDa9H Eric H told me about the article, so I've read it, thank you. I might have to read it again – it really is a lot to take in. Narrowing the list down to 30, imo, makes it a bit unfair. Most certainly there would be 100 living songwriters worthy of mention, right? But it's definitely a fun read. Thanks for posting it!

@sotto voce
Many of the many comments were interesting.   Among the missing living, commenters were most upset about Billy Joel not being on the list.  I think I agree. And until I refocused on the American part, I was getting really steamed that Joni was not there.

@Vaer

Oh, I forgot to read the comments!
I would have to agree that Billy Joel should not have been left out. 
I hadn't caught on that it was only Americans. Such an important detail! That explains why Sting didn't make the list.

@Vaer

Very cool. Thank you!
@Aaron Hello!

@all

Yeah. I finished in under 50% of my average.  Whatever average means given the clock starts the first time you ever attempt a Friday. 

But I savored every second. I didn’t rush.  Yes it was ‘quick’. But ride was so satisfying.  As other have said Weintraub-esque.   Smooth like melted butter.  

I’ll take fast and smooth, to slow and incongruent.  Every. Single. Time.

@Katie

Inside No. 9 was (is?) such a great show! I think S2 E2 is my all-time fav, but unfortunately I never watched beyond S3. I watched back when I still used a DVD delivery service; now I don't own a device that can play a disc of any kind, and it isn't on Netflix.

@Bill in Yokohama Aww, nice to see a fellow fan! The following seasons are great too, but it's nearly impossible to watch right now unless you're in the UK or can play DVDs. Hope it comes back somewhere soon!

@Matt

How Weintraubian? Robyn would never let slide such pedestrian clueing.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@SP

Sticks in locks = OARS
Some winds = OBOES
Announcements before giveaways? = SPOILERALERTS

Very Weintraub adjacent or -lite, if you ask me.

And the 11 minutes it took me were leisurely, two-fingered, reclined on my left elbow with a clunky wireless keyboard and battling a blind, overweight, purring cat for lap space.

No bueno for a Friday "challenge."

@Matt

Sticks in locks, yes. SPOILERALERT, sure. But far too few other really good misdirections, and maybe I’m just jaded but “some winds” for OBOES didn’t throw me for a second and even “Plants overseas” was almost a gimme except AGENT before ASSET.

@Matt

I've always found Rena Cohen puzzles to be Ezerskian rather than anything else 🤷🏽‍♂️

@Matt Embarrassingly easy. A PR for me, slightly slower than my Monday average.

If the editors want us to go elsewhere for a satisfying challenge, please just say so. Let’s not pretend they’re not dumbing the puzzle down dramatically and really just destroying a decades-long legacy.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon

commentBody: @Matt For consistently difficult puzzles, try: Newsday Saturday Stumper https://brainsonly.com/global/netword/cwd/#/s/260501 This links to today's puzzle with the wrong date on top. Only Saturday is truly challenging. Every other Wednesday, Stella Zawistowski https://inteltainment.org/

commentBody:
@Nancy J.

Thanks!

@Andrzej

Agree with you about gem vs gem cut. MISSES (perhaps a little outdated these days) is the section that sells clothing for young women—as if anyone cares these days.

Thanks. The 1950s called? But why in a puzzle by a woman much younger than me?

My wife and I usually shop for clothes together. I've never seen age as section division criteria in a dress shop. A section for kids in a huge clothing shop? Sure. A section for young ladies in a dress shop, specifically? Never.

@Andrzej

Other typical sections are Juniors (teens) and Womens, which flank Misses, and you might also find Petites, for those shorter than 160 Cm.

@Andrzej When I was a teenager and wore dresses, you had three main categories of sizes: junior, miss, and women. If I remember correctly, juniors were sized as odd numbers (11, 13, etc.) and misses were even numbers (12, 14, etc.), and I think misses were sized for taller girls, though I might be wrong about that because there was a subset, junior petite, for us shorties. The women's clothes were sized for the 'mature' figure. Sewing patterns followed the same convention.

@Andrzej   My "retirement" job was in a clothing store, so I can answer this: there are three categories for adult clothing: Petites for women 5'4" and under, Women's are the plus sizes, and Misses are the mainstream sizes 0 - 16.  They can often be the same style, like a shirt, but cut differently.
Juniors are the odd sizes 1-11 for teens and young women. They are completely separate styles from the adult items.

@Chris

Apparently we label everything differently over here.

@Andrzej. The Juniors section of a department store contains clothes mostly for teenage girls, after they have outgrown the children's department.  Misses sections are usually the largest in a department store, with scores of designer collections to account for. This sizing caters to mature women with developed figures and more curves than those of Juniors. Garments in Women's sizing are made for those with a larger, taller build and a desire for clothing that is comfortable and loose as to not restrict movement.  This is all US lingo.

@Kris H

Yeah, apparently it's one of those things you do differently in America than we do here. Thanks for elaborating.

@Andrzej
Please! I'm still recovering from the discovery that I took a size 43 shoe!

@Andrzej

MISSES often is next to the
“Ladies” section
and offers plus-sized options.

@Kris H
Sounds complicated! In the UK there is just a "Women" department, and no other categories. We all get our jeans from the same place. 

Maybe because there is not the same volume of sales as in the US, there is more of a one-size-fits-all approach. And after all, both 14 year-olds and 50 year-olds come in all sizes!

There is now 'vanity sizing' - clothes that some years ago would be a UK 14 are now sized as 12 or even 10.

@Jane Wheelaghan

Right? Same here.

@Andrzej

MISSES are what they used to call the standard  even numbered sizes. Juniors are  the younger cuts in the odd number sizes. They were (are?) cut slimmer in the hips. I don’t know if they still exist.

Andrzej,
In a department store, not a dress shop.

@Andrzej

It’s not an age thing. Just sizing.

Teens and juniors tend to be smaller, 
but ladies are generally size 6-12.

Sizes 14-22 (or so) are in a separate section. 
When I was pregnant 
I far preferred that section
to the dreaded maternity disasters
with poofy sleeves and bows.

@CCNY

How is calling sizes by age groups not and age thing?

@Andrzej
In my experience, the MISSES section (or Juniors, as it's usually called now) had clothes that were more "hip" than in the Women's section. When I was a teen, that meant the MISSES section was full of low-rise jeans with holes in them, and the Women's section was full of mom jeans with waists up to the rib cage.

@Francis
No, the teens and tweens are "Juniors"...and the "more mature" ladies are shopping in the "Women's" department.  The tailoring is a bit different from that for the MISSES.

@Andrzej - It's an older term for women's clothes seen a lot in the old big mail-order catalogs from Sears and JC Penny's.  They were large department stores in the US.  Juniors were for teens.  Misses were for women 20 and up who still had a waist.  Women's was for "mature" women who had put on some pounds and was less fashionable.

@Andrzej

The sizes (6-12, 14 and up) do not refer to any ages.

I don’t shop there in my 50’s, 
but I did in my 20’s.

I wear an 8 and I am in my 50’s.

Does that make more sense?

@CCNY

Not really. Mine and yours frames of reference seem to be completely different.

@Mean Old Lady

Yes, I really blew that one, didn't I?

@JayTee 

Per Merriam Webster, "baguette" can also refer to the stone itself.

@Apurv
Bump ON A LOG and LOCK AND LOAD are both very well known idioms in English. MASA, which I believe is a corn flour used for Mexican cooking, is an ethnically specific item but has appeared here before. And Tom DOOLEY was a hit folk song but in another era in the last century.
So what does "bump on a log" mean?
@Andrzej it’s an expression to describe someone who sits around doing nothing: like a bump on a log.

@Andrzej

It’s a simile for something that doesn’t move or do anything. “He just sat there like a bump on a log.”
@Andrzej  I think originally someone who is present but very quiet, but it’s become more of an expression for being useless—someone who may be present in a situation but not doing anything to help.

@MB

Ah, so like Congress in stopping autocracy.

@Andrzej

In my head,
BUMP ON A LOG
is *always* heard in my dad’s voice.

And he left us in 1991.

That’s how often he said it.
@Andrzej Yes. Very much so.

@Chris

It wasn't easy for me.  I suspected that the plants in question wouldn't involve gardening, but I thought it was going to be about factories, not spying.

@ED

Well done!

And lovely that you decided to chime in!

Welcome to the chat.

We’re a mixed bag,
which only makes new recruits
that much more valuable!
@ED Welcome!

@ED I loved turtle rabbit! it made me sad to live in a place that will provide so few opportunities to casually drop it into conversation. It’ll have to wait until my next visit to see the fam in Texas. “Oh look,” I’ll say, “a turtle rabbit.”

@Brad  I don’t see why this is a mistake. Oar locks can be referred to as just “locks”—allowing the clue to throw us off—locks could mean hair (people use hair sticks in updos) or canal locks or door locks (which was my first thought).

Brad,
Yes, many hours ago, and several times since then. 
Or was your question rhetorical?

@MB

It's a mistake because Brad's interpretation is the most obvious.  Rowboats necessarily have oarlocks; they aren't necessarily in canals.  Using 5A's razor, the oarlocks are the "horses," and the canal locks are the "zebras," which is to say that you go with the more obvious interpretation.

@MB

The "mistake" isn't with the clue in the puzzle, but with Sean McGowan's interpretation of it in the Wordplay column.

@Barry Ancona

The snark is not welcome.

@Elbridge Gerry
Yes, but the principle is correct. If you have to ask, "Has anyone already...", around here the answer is always yes. No excuse now that we have (Glory be!) a search button, rickety though it is.

@Elbridge Gerry

“Has anyone already pointed out?” = “I’m too lazy to look for myself.”

@MB
btw Wordplay did consider it a mistake, and published a correction.

Heidi,
As per yesterday's comments, you are not the only one who successfully completed the puzzle without noticing the hooks (films) or the fish. That can happen on any Tricky Thursday when the "trick" is not key to solving the puzzle, but is, rather, an added attraction to it.

@Barry Ancona

But that makes it not a very Tricky Thursday, no? I just meant that making the trick a bonus feature, rather than integral to the solve, takes away a lot of the enjoyment. I want to work for my Thursdays, not just admire them.

@Joe P

And thanks to your post I just realized I've been thinking of Mike's Munster as if it were the German Münster, and pronouncing it accordingly (in my head).

@Andrzej
I have been assuming that Mike IS in Münster, given that he posts so early...

@Mean Old Lady
I've assumed it was a lieu de plume.

@Marshall Walthew
How about Phil Ochs? We need him now. For my money, Springsteen's anthem doesn't measure up. YMMV.

@Times Rita

I was/am into Phil Ochs, but did not come to him through my dad’s music.

@Times Rita
I miss Ochs too, but Springsteen has a song about the USA with the lines:
  Things we'll do
  And things we won't 

An all-too-apt anthem for these times, don't you think?
@Marcy I think it means that good crossword people aren’t snarky cheats and appreciate a self sacrifice to allow others to enjoy something.

@Marcy

Rena's father sacrificed his score so her puzzle could be used in the tournament.  This showed his intergrity, not solving a puzzle he had already seen, and it lowered his overall total as a result.

@Jane Wheelaghan In US dorms sharing a room is still common. Most universities don't have enough dorm space to accommodate the incoming freshmen class in single rooms. Oddly, in her freshman year, my daughter requested a double because she was worried about making friends. The university didn't have enough doubles to accommodate everyone who wanted them, so she was "forced" into a single. She loved it, and she had no trouble making friends.
@Jane Wheelaghan yes, its the norm in the US and I’m sure many other places

@Andrew - don’t be so sure.  Seems to be not the norm in Europe and I can say definitively it is not the case in Canada.  I was surprised too, but my kid visited about 8 school in Canada this year and doubles are rare and often available for those looking to save money.

@Lewis Totally agree about junk-scarce. Not a single OH I SEE or I HOPE NOT. I hope this is a trend.

I also really wanted PUNSTERS to somehow be a word for crossword puzzle creators.

@The X-Phile
I *do* call it Tar-zhay! although I also like Le Dot and The Bullseye Boutique.

@JohnWM
LOL  I thought about the ANTS ON A LOG, too.  I suspect that was behind the appearance of this puzzle right on the heels of that other puzzle....

And because I have a two-year-old granddaughter (as an excuse):

There’s an AEROBE on the SNAKE on the NAPE of the WAG with their hand on the stick in the lock as they search for the CIA agent who is now missing AFOOT and basically just a bump on a log in a hole in the bottom of the seeeea.
There’s an aerobe on the snake on the nape (everyone! …… … ..

Mean Old Lady,

That NW corner had me noticing lots of organisms on display: the bumps on a log (be they ants or burls), human aerobes on a romcom, rabbits on turtles (oh my!), and I’m assuming the back-stabbing snakes on a plane.
:)

@Xword Junkie

"Turtle-rabbit" wasn't a gimme for me, but even if it had been, it would still be a clever, amusing clue.

@The X-Phile

But I don't want clever or amusing on Friday, I want difficult!

Katie,

I wonder if an AI is currently scouring the archive for too-tricky clues and rewriting them - to make the historical collection future-friendly with more ma$$ appeal?

@JohnWM

Interesting conspiracy theory…but I doubt it.

The only way that would be practical (unless the NYT is just going absolutely hog-wild with “AI” and simply trusting its judgment) is if the NYT collected some pretty fine-grained metrics regarding individual puzzlers’ solving journeys.

Which is to say: how else would you identify “too-tricky clues” with any semblance of reliability?

@Joe
That song was so popular!  And I never could understand why.  I think it was on my sister's first LP (featuring The Kingston Trio--heart-throbs!)  We listened to that record at least once a day.
@Joe -- the real story of Tom Dula: https://www.ncarts.org/blog/2019/10/29/legend-behind-north-carolinas-most-famous-murder-ballad
Agreed: SONOROUS is woody. Not at all tinny.
@Nancy Lovely words. My personal favorites are petrichor and susseration.

@Michelle Botwinick
SussUration. And yes, it is a lovely word.

Seems most would disagree about the putative 'uptick'.
Friday PB for me, e.g.
@Charles Peterson oh gosh a PB for me too! Five minutes below Friday average. Sigh.

@Mean Old Lady
The CIA does not have agents, they have case officers, who develop and manage ASSETs. If anyone ever tells you they were a CIA agent, they were probably a clerk.

@cameron Not that I plan on competing, but I'm curious. Do most solvers accept their gold star and streak continuation after reading the Word Play column, or is it half-cheating? Do we need to add silver and bronze stars for using a hint?

All's in good fun!

@Phil
 Good luck!
@Phil you got this

@Phil
Please provide an update tomorrow! Best of luck!

@Phil
You can, but please don't. There's plenty to aim for beyond an immaculate week.
@Gerry also, I did consider that "baguettes" are a cut, not gems themselves. But, I don't need to pick that nit.

The editors routinely change many of the clues in every puzzle, and have always done so. Constructors submit a grid of answers and their proposed clues. Nobody is saying the answers are too easy ... and the constructors may well have submitted harder clues; in some cases, they have said so.
jennie — I can assure you that the editors have a virtually limitless supply of hard puzzles that they can use if they want to.
@Dan Yes I think the dial can go up pretty easily!
@Niall 142 seconds, as in 2 min, 22 sec? That's hardly enough time to type the answers in.
Nah, it's the time posting I'm laughing at, not the too easy gang.

@Matt
It’s called the reveal puzzle button.

@Niall

Fair enough. I guess I tend to assume that there is nearly complete overlap between the sets of folks who would object to either of those two things…but perhaps not!

That said, I’ve never understood why people would want to post their actual solve times (as opposed to, e.g., % diff from their own daily average) in this forum.

Also, having read it again, I do like your language about being “bogged down for a while in various cardinal directions”.

All of that being said…the puzzles are too damn easy!

Matt,
"...reading different clues at the same time with each eye..." suggested parody to me.

@Barry Ancona
I imagined Niall as some gecko-like being with one eye turned northeast in its socket and the other southwest, typing with all four feet. Ireland is across the sea but it's not another planet.

@jim

Right?
@jimUnless maybe "race" is taken to mean a race event -- a day of racing -- rather than a single contest.  Maybe?

@jim
When the competition (track race) has more entrants than lanes I believe that they have several “heats”.  Winners from each heat then compete in the final “heat” which determines the winner. Just my observation.

@jim, it is definitely a race, but it's a race WITHIN a race. "Heat" denotes that's it's part of a bigger race. The top times from all heats combined move on. Winning a heat doesn't guarantee advancement if someone in another heat beats your time.

@Lynn
I had bicS originally, as in sticking a biro in your locks behind your ear.

@Niall  Top o' the morning!  (for me)   I had to look up "biro" as it is new to me.  An Irish (and UK) term referring to Laszlo Biro the Hungarian born Argentinian inventor (patentee) of the first ball point pen.  Hence (the incorrect answer) "bics."

I feel like I've traveled.

@Dale M
Haha, it didn’t occur to me that biro wouldn’t be universal. I’m used to it being the other way around here.

@Alexandra Dixon

Oh my gosh! Yikes!!! 

That sounds so miserable! I'll definitely take my former roommate's glaring silence and accusations over that! 

And to quote a puzzle from the last few days, regarding asking you for funds: WHATNERVE! (And I work in development at a university.)

@Vaer
I agree, and I guess I have Show Overlays on bc I saw it and loved it. I feel bad for my husband who prints out all his puzzles and never sees that kind of thing unless I show him. His choice though. He still likes reading paper newspapers. Silly man. (I wanted to do a funny face emoji but I haven't figured out how to do that on my Mac.  Any advice? I can do them on my iPhone. I need to get my teenage grandson to help me I guess.

@William Kash, when all racers cannot compete at the same time, they must run in heats. Think of the Olympics. There are many heats for each race.

You say, "to (your) mind", but that doesn't make it so. I ran track in high school; it's definitely called a heat. The meet is the entirety of the competition, which typically includes many events, not all of which are races.
